question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
literature, in this systematic review, we sought to determine
the validity of the causality relationship via the use of one of
two (or both) most common protocols for adverse drug reactions:
challenge-dechallenge-rechallenge (C-D-R) protocol
and/or the Adverse Drug Reactions Probability Scale (APS),
also known as Naranjo algorithm . However, different algorithms,
such as theWHO-UMC system,Gardnermethod,
Jones algorithm, or Kramer questionnaire, currently validated
in the scientific literature to evaluate ADRs , were
also considered if used in the included papers. Additionally, we
evaluated the complete or partial resolution of the oral lesions
upon withdrawal without treatment and the most common alleged
offending medication(s).

The systematic review included the following: (i) articles
(case reports, case series, cohort studies, and case-control)
published between January 1966 and December 2016 in the
English language, (ii) cases with clinical features of DIOLRs
with or without cutaneous and/or other mucous membranes
involvement, (iii) only cases where diagnosis of DIOLRs was
based on a histopathological examination (hematoxylin and
eosin stain (H&E)) via an oral biopsy with or without a direct
immunofluorescence (DIF) analysis, (iv) cases where the culprit
medication(s) was/were clearly stated, and (v) cases in
which oral lesions appeared during or after administration of
systemic medications only.
We excluded randomized and non-randomized trials because
they are interventional in nature and cross-sectional
studies as they are performed at a single point in time or time
interval, so unsuitable to establish a possible causative relationship.
We also excluded case(s) with (i) oral involvement
but no oral biopsy was taken, (ii) oral and cutaneous
involvement with only a cutaneous biopsy or where the
location of the biopsy was not clearly specified (skin viz.
oral), (iii) only cutaneous lesions, (iv) oral lichenoid reactions
induced by dental materials, and (v) preexisting OLP/
OLLs

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers:
We performed a computerized
literature search for articles published in nine databases from
January 1966 to December 2016: Ovid MEDLINE, Embase,
CINAHL, Scopus, Web of Science, Cochrane Libraries,
ProQuest, ClinicalTrials.gov, and Google Scholar

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
In addition, all references retrieved from all included articles
were further examined manually to identify other relevant
articles that were not detected by the initial search.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers:
The
searches were concentrated on the two main topics of oral
lichen planus/oral lichenoid lesions/oral lichenoid reactions
and the adverse effects of medication, and drug-induced. We
searched these concepts using controlled vocabulary particular
to each database and keywords. MeSH terms included, but not
limited to, Lichen Planus, Oral, Lichenoid lesions, Lichenoid
reactions, Lichenoid eruptions AND Mouth, and Drug Related
Side Effects and Adverse Reactions. The Ovid MEDLINE
search was altered for each database. Citations and abstracts
of retrieved articles were exported into RefWorks, and duplicates
were removed. The complete search strategies for the
three main database Ovid MEDLINE, Embase, and Scopus
are included in the Appendix.

answers:

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
After eliminating duplicates, two investigators (GF and JHS)
independently evaluated titles, abstract and full text of all the
included articles. Any disagreement was discussed and resolved
by consensus between the two investigators.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
Studies that met the inclusion criteria were independently
reviewed by two investigators (GF and JS) who extracted
and tabulated in an Excel sheet the following information

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
The characteristics of all included case reports/series and studies
are described in Tables 1, 2, and 3.

In case reports/series, there was a slightly higher frequency
in the females (53.7%) than males (44.4%), with a mean age
of 56.81 (SD 11.72) and the highest number of cases reported
in Australia (25.5%) followed by USA (19.1%) and Italy
(14.9%) (Table 1).
The median number of culprit medication(s) described was
1 (Q1-Q3, 1-3) (Table 1), with the most frequent ones being
Methyldopa with 11 cases (20.37%), Interferon (IFN)-alpha
with 6 cases (11.11%), and Imatinib and Infliximab with 5
cases (9.26%) (Table 2).
The median number of concurrent medication(s) described
was 2 (Q1-Q3, 1-3) (Table 1), with the most frequent ones
being Potassium chloride with 5 cases (14.29%) and
Allopurinol and vitamin C with 3 cases (8.57%) (Table 2).
Interestingly, only 8 (14.8) cases showed no concurrent
medications other than the culprit, whereas in 27 other cases,
1 to 7 concurrent medications were reported (Table 1).
Similarly, in only 43 (83.3%) of cases, there was one single
culprit medication, while in the remaining 9 cases (16.37%), it
varied from 2 to 4 (Table 1).
Additional information about the frequency of cutaneous
and other mucous membrane involvement, history of personal
and family allergy, and direct immunofluorescence (DIF) are
also described in Table 3.

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
As shown in Fig. 1, we selected 155 full-text articles out of
810 screened. Of those 155, only 46 were included for qualitative
synthesis: 40 case reports/case series, to a total of 54
patients described, and 6 studies

Table 1 Demographics,
medications, and timing of
latency and partial/complete resolution
of oral lesions on 54
DIOLR case reports

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
only 46 were included for qualitative
synthesis

Descriptive statistics were calculated on 54 patients with
DIOLRs described in 40 case reports/series, including
mean +- standard deviation for age, medians with interquartile
range for timing of latency period, partial/complete resolution
of oral lesions, and last follow-up, and frequency
distribution for offending and concurrent medication distribution,
clinical characteristics, and algorithms used for adverse
drug reactions.
Mann-Whitney U test was used to determine the difference
of medians of latency period and time partial/complete resolution
of oral lesions between males (M) and females (F), whereas
Spearman's correlation (r) was used to identify any correlation
between age and latency period and between age and
time partial/complete resolution of oral lesions. Conversely,
Kruskal-Wallis analysis was used to evaluate the difference
of medians between age, latency period, and time partial/
complete resolution of oral lesions by number of concurrent
mediation(s).

answers:

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: nan

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Our review has several limitations, mainly related to the fact
that our analysis is only based on case reports, since the included
studies presented with incomplete data for an accurate
analysis regarding drugs and OLLs. Because of their nature,
those case reports are unable per se of depicting an accurate
clinical overview of this entity. Case reports represent the
lowest level of scientific evidence; they can be biased by the
authors as well Fig. 3 Histopathological features: word cloud of the most frequent terms as reviewers who based their assumption on
previous similar reports, which might not have used any of the
many standardized protocol/algorithm to assess an ADR.
Another important limitation is that those included cases
were not homogenous: not all cases described information
regarding all the variables tabulated in Tables 7 and 8.
Additionally,many possible cases of DIOLRs might ormight
not have been reported only because previous reports on the
same medication had already been described in the literature.
We excluded all those muco-cutaneous cases where an oral
biopsy was not performed. This might have introduced a bias
in reporting other cases of DIOLRs; however, it would have
been impossible to truly ascertain the nature of the oral lesions,
without a histopathological confirmation from an oral
biopsy. If this had been performed in all muco-cutaneous
cases, we would have probably had a different number of
cases to be included in our analysis.
Lastly, we still do not have standardized and validated diagnostic
criteria for DIOLRs; there is no consensus statement
on this entity. Therefore, if such criteria had existed, some of
those case reports would have not fit those criteria, while some
others would, thus making the total number of cases included
in this review overrepresented or underrepresented.
